title: Adam Ferguson on population and wealth
abstract: In small, isolated communities it is self-evident that population size is linked to collective well-being, even to survival, hence it is likely to be the object of intrusive social control. In populous societies, in contrast, the collective demographic interest is harder to discern and even where governments have demographic objectives in mind the links to individual behavior are tenuous. Those links first received attention by scholars in early modern Europe, a period and region in which governments imbued with mercantilist thinking often sought to devise pronatalist and pro-immigration measures (an assortment described by Jeremy Bentham as a political pharmacopoeia). Skepticism about the worth of such measures was no doubt usually justified. As some were coming to recognize, the far more important influences on demographic outcomes lay in the conditions of social and economic life that govern the opportunities and aspirations of families and individuals. Those conditions are certainly affected by much that governments do, if typically with no demographic intent, but they are also set by the market economy and, not least, by the nongovernment, nonmarket domain that is known as civil society. Among early writers who treat the topic of societal influences on demographic behavior, Montesquieu is the most prominent. Also worthy of attention, a generation after Montesquieu and showing his influence, is the Scottish philosopher and historian Adam Ferguson (1723 - 1816). Ferguson, professor of natural philosophy and, later, moral philosophy at the University of Edinburgh, was a significant figure in the Scottish Enlightenment - a contemporary of David Hume and Adam Smith. His best-known work, An Essay on the History of Civil Society ( 1767), traces the course of social development from small-scale rude or barbarous subsistence societies to the large, complex, and relatively prosperous states of his own region and time. One chapter of the Essay, reprinted below, deals with the subject of population - a topic with which, as he disarmingly puts it, he is not much conversant, and still less engaged. But while somewhat repetitive, the account is never dull: it is full of sharp insights and memorable turns of phrase. Ferguson explores how societies and states maintain or increase their populations - the new laissez faire economics of Smith and others, like the mercantilism it was supplanting, favored population increase. It is hard, he writes, to find any instances of wise or effectual measures to increase population; most, if not wholly inept, simply tamper with palliatives. All that was needed to achieve the desired result was to ensure the security, liberty, and ( thence) prosperity of their citizens; with those in place, governments should stand aside. The chapter also contains a sophisticated discussion of the effect of rising consumption norms on marriage and fertility, more subtle than that given in Sir James Steuart's Principles of Political Oeconomy (published in the same year) and prefiguring that in Malthus's Second Essay of 1803. In an especially modern touch, Ferguson sees wants as insatiable: it is the continual increase of riches, not any measure attained, that keeps the craving imagination at ease. The excerpt is the whole of Section IV of Part III (pp. 210 - 223), entitled Of Population and Wealth, taken from the first edition of the Essay. ( The revisions in the second and later editions leave this chapter little changed.).
